one_line_takeaway: EchoSafe shows that a training-free, self-reflective memory that accumulates and retrieves past safety insights at inference time can substantially reduce both jailbreak success and over-defensive refusals on the paper's own new contextual-safety benchmark, MM-SafetyBench++, at modest (1.3-1.7x) compute overhead.
key_finding: On MM-SafetyBench++ (GEN mode), EchoSafe consistently beats all three baselines across all six categories and three backbone models; on Qwen-2.5-VL it reaches an average CCR of 87.9%, beating AdaShield by 16.8 points, while AdaShield's high refusal rate (e.g. 78-97% RR) comes with severe over-defense (answer rates on safe queries collapsing to as low as 31-45%). On the original MM-SafetyBench, EchoSafe with Qwen-2.5-VL cuts ASR from the base model's 22.72%/25.05% (SD/TYPO) to 0.04%/0.02%. On MSSBench-Chat with Qwen-2.5-VL it improves average accuracy by 18.75 points over base; on SIUO it gains 27.04% (Safe) and 20.83% (Reasoning) over competing methods. Continual memory accumulation across categories yields up to +5.2% additional CCR (Figure 3) without re-initializing the memory, evidencing cross-domain transfer of stored insights. Efficiency: EchoSafe adds only 1.33x inference time and 1.69x total FLOPs versus the base model while delivering a 2.60x CCR improvement (Figure 4), and utility on MME/MMBench/ScienceQA/TextVQA stays close to the unmodified base model. Safety-fine-tuned baselines (Post-hoc LoRA, Mixed LoRA) achieve near-100% refusal on unsafe queries but answer rates collapse to 0-5% on safe queries, empirically confirming the safety-utility trade-off the paper argues static/fine-tuned defenses cannot escape.
core_contribution: The paper (Zhang, He, He, Sycara, Xie; CMU Robotics Institute) makes two own contributions, kept distinct from cited prior work: (1) MM-SafetyBench++, a contextual-safety benchmark built by taking the malicious query set from MM-SafetyBench and, for each unsafe image-text query, generating a paired 'safe' counterpart via minimal GPT-5-assisted rewrites that flip user intent while preserving contextual framing (e.g. 'evading taxes without getting caught' -> 'reducing tax liabilities using legal tax-planning strategies'). Images for both subsets are synthesized/edited with Qwen-Image under two attack modes, GEN (image-generated intent) and GENOCR (text embedded naturally in the scene). (2) EchoSafe, a training-free, inference-time defense that equips an MLLM with a growing self-reflective memory bank: after each response the model performs self-reflection to distill a generalizable 'safety insight' (Eq. 3), stores it with a CLIP-based joint text+image context embedding (Eq. 4), and on future queries retrieves the top-k most similar past insights (cosine similarity, Eq. 6) to inject as extra prompt context before generating a response (closed-loop memory update, Eq. 2/5/7). No model weights are modified.
threat_model: The paper targets MLLM 'contextual safety' failures rather than a single attack technique: (a) overdefensiveness, where safety-aligned/fine-tuned MLLMs refuse benign queries whose surface features (e.g. presence of a knife) superficially resemble unsafe ones; and (b) contextual jailbreaks, where models fail to refuse genuinely harmful image-text queries that are visually realistic (GEN, using a strong text-to-image generator) or embed the harmful intent as in-scene text requiring OCR (GENOCR), rather than the crude typographic overlays used in prior benchmarks like MM-SafetyBench. The defender is the MLLM itself at inference time; the attacker supplies image-text pairs across six OpenAI-policy categories (illegal activity, hate speech, malware generation, physical harm, fraud, sex). No adversarial perturbation / gradient-based attack setting is considered; the focus is benign-vs-malicious intent discrimination under naturalistic multimodal inputs.
limitations: The paper's own text (main body + first two appendix sections read) does not include a dedicated limitations paragraph in the portion extracted, though the introduction/discussion repeatedly frames limitations of prior benchmarks (not of EchoSafe itself) around coarse ASR-only metrics and lack of paired safe/unsafe data. Observable methodological limitations from the described design: (1) the memory bank is initialized empty per safety category in the main experiments, so first-encounter performance on a novel category is not isolated from the reported averages -- the reported gains partly reflect within-run accumulation over the evaluation stream itself, which is somewhat optimistic relative to a true zero-history deployment; (2) the 'safe' rewrites and quality judgments both depend on GPT-5/gpt-5-mini, creating a risk of judge-model bias or blind spots shared between benchmark construction and evaluation, partially mitigated only by the reported cross-model (rho=0.72) and human (rho=0.74) agreement checks, which still leave meaningful residual disagreement; (3) EchoSafe requires an embedding-based similarity search over a growing memory and self-reflection generation per turn, so cost grows with deployment history despite the reported 1.33x/1.69x overhead being measured only in the paper's own benchmark-scale setting; (4) evaluation is restricted to three relatively small (7-8B) open-source backbones for the EchoSafe method itself, with GPT-5/Gemini included only as reference points on the static benchmark, not as EchoSafe hosts.

datasets_used: MM-SafetyBench++ (paper's own contribution; GEN and GENOCR attack modes; 6 categories: Illegal Activity, Hate Speech, Malware Generation, Physical Harm, Fraud, Sex)
MM-SafetyBench (Liu et al., original, used both as the base for MM-SafetyBench++ and as a standalone eval with SD/TYPO/SD-TYPO attack modes)
MME, MMBench, ScienceQA, TextVQA (general-purpose utility benchmarks)
quantitative_results: MM-SafetyBench++ image quality vs original MM-SafetyBench: CLIPScore 0.21->0.25, BLIP2Score 0.52->0.67, Inception Score 13.53->8.32, ASR 22.72%->41.04%
EchoSafe on Qwen-2.5-VL, MM-SafetyBench++ GEN mode: average CCR 87.9%, beating AdaShield by 16.8 points
EchoSafe on Qwen-2.5-VL, MM-SafetyBench: ASR reduced from base 22.72%/25.05% (SD/TYPO) to 0.04%/0.02%
EchoSafe on Qwen-2.5-VL, MSSBench-Chat: +18.75% average accuracy over base model
EchoSafe on SIUO: +27.04% (Safe) and +20.83% (Reasoning) over competing methods
Memory accumulation ablation (Qwen-2.5-VL, MM-SafetyBench++): up to +5.2% CCR from continual (non-reinitialized) memory across categories
Efficiency vs baselines (Qwen-2.5-VL): 1.33x inference time, 1.69x total FLOPs, 2.60x CCR improvement
Judge reliability: Spearman rho=0.72 (gpt-5-mini vs Gemini-2.5-Flash), rho=0.74 (gpt-5-mini vs human annotators)
Safety fine-tuned baselines (Post-hoc/Mixed LoRA on LLaVA-1.5-7B) achieve ~100% refusal rate on unsafe queries but answer rate on safe queries collapses to 0-5.5% across categories
baselines_compared: ECSO (Eyes Closed, Safety On -- image-to-text transformation defense)
Post-hoc LoRA and Mixed LoRA (safety fine-tuning baselines)
Proprietary/open-source reference models on the static benchmark only (not run with EchoSafe): GPT-5, GPT-5-Mini, GPT-4o-Mini, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Qwen3-VL-8B, InternVL3.5-8B
